evidence_quotes.quote: prohibiting the removal or planned obsolescence of a consumers purchased product
online services for "The Crew" and it's subsequent removal from storefronts
the announcement of the shutdown was only a few months prior while still keeping it available for purchase
they effectively made any purchases of the game or in relation to it, i.e. DLC, moot.
Second, I think that this act should also prohibit payment processors, such as VISA and Mastercard, from determining what consumers can spend their money on.
There have been numerous instances of these payment processors threatening to remove their services from businesses hosting content they find distasteful, or think could hurt their "brand image".

This has happened most recently to Steam and itch.io, where due to an extremely small group's complaints to VISA and Mastercard, which then told the platforms to remove the offending content, lest they cease providing their service, and so numerous titles were either removed outright or delisted from their respective platforms.

All in all I think this act is a step in the right direction, however I think these two topics would also be good additions to this legislation, because in my view these are pretty simple consumer rights.

paragraphs.text: Greeting! I would support the current form of this act, however I would like to address two topics here which I think would be good additions to this act. First, I think this act would be made better by prohibiting the removal or planned obsolescence of a consumers purchased product, which can most notably be observed in video games, where most recently Ubisoft stopped online services for "The Crew" and it's subsequent removal from storefronts, which wouldn't be a problem in and of itself, however the announcement of the shutdown was only a few months prior while still keeping it available for purchase, despite them likely that any consumers that bought it wouldn't be able to play it, as the owners of the game found out, for some reason the shutdown of online services meant that all gameplay functionality would cease even though the game had a gameplay mode that did not need it. Therefore, they effectively made any purchases of the game or in relation to it, i.e. DLC, moot. I also have a personal anecdote of this issue where I absolutely loved the game "Driver: San Francisco" by Ubisoft when I was younger, however a couple years ago I found out that it was no longer available for purchase, even digitally, which I found very odd, since, in my mind at least, there is little to no cost for a publisher or developer to keep a digital copy of a game available for purchase. Because of that, in order to play it I would have to either find and purchase an old copy of the game from a third party or pirate the game. These games are obviously not the only ones shut down by their developer or publisher and unless such a practice isn't prohibited they won't be the last. All that I would ask of this provision would mostly just be the same thing the ECI "Stop Destroying Videogames" is asking for. Second, I think that this act should also prohibit payment processors, such as VISA and Mastercard, from determining what consumers can spend their money on. There's been numerous instances of these payment processors threatening to remove their services from businesses hosting content they find distasteful, or think could hurt their "brand image". This has happened most recently to Steam and itch.io, where due to an extremely small group's complaints to VISA and Mastercard, which then told the platforms to remove the offending content, lest they cease providing their service, and so numerous titles were either removed outright or delisted from their respective platforms. All these complaints were made to games which depicted fictional adult topics, although so far the majority that have suffered are explicitly sexual games, mostly including incestuous or non-consensual themes, however games with psychological themes, such as "Detroit: Become Human" were also targeted. Previous instances of this happened to Patreon and Pixiv, which both host fictional adult, i.e. games, animations and other forms of art. All in all I think this act is a step in the right direction, however I think these two topics would also be good additions to this legislation, because in my view these are pretty simple consumer rights. Thank you kindly for reading.
